# Runbook — ITS_Errors triage (reading the error log + the mark-resolved / clear verbs) (Successor-Remediation, Op Stds §43)

<!-- BEGIN TREE-XREF (generated by scripts/build_runbook_xrefs.py — do not edit inside) -->
> **In the troubleshooting tree** ([printable guide](../troubleshooting/troubleshooting_guide.md) · dashboard `/troubleshoot`). This runbook resolves:
>   - The daemon plane — liveness, breaker, alerts, row-cap, picklists, guards → Heartbeats + watchdog Check C keep daemons honest → “There are open CRITICAL errors.”
> Daemon(s): `watchdog` — see the [daemon reference](../references/daemon_reference.md).
<!-- END TREE-XREF -->

A §43 successor-remediation entry, written for the **Successor-Operator**: a trained
operator who runs Claude Code and reads Smartsheet rows + alert emails, but does **not**
read code. The §42 code-reader rationale lives in `shared/error_log.py` (the triple-fire
legs + recursion guards) and `operator_dashboard/act/errors_ops.py` (the two dashboard
verbs and their invariants).

## Purpose

`ITS_Errors` (ITS — System workspace / "02 — Logs") is the system-wide error log:
**every daemon and script writes one row per noteworthy event** there, and it is the
first sheet to read when anything looks wrong. This runbook explains how to read a row,
what an "open CRITICAL" is and why it matters, and the two low-class dashboard repairs —
**mark-resolved** and **clear** — with their escalation boundary.

## What one row means

The columns you triage on:

- **Error** — a short, stable error code (e.g. `po_creds_missing`,
  `uncaught_exception`, `portal_pending_fetch_sustained`). The same fault fires the
  same code every time, so the code is how you recognize a *class* of problem — and it
  is one of the two filter axes the dashboard mark-resolved verb accepts.
- **Script** — the dotted name of the daemon/script that logged it (e.g.
  `safety_reports.portal_poll`, `scripts.watchdog`). The other filter axis.
- **Severity** — one of **INFO / WARN / ERROR / CRITICAL**:
  - **INFO** — routine breadcrumbs; mostly kept out of the sheet.
  - **WARN** — something degraded but the work continued (a skipped row, a
    best-effort mirror miss). Read it; usually no action.
  - **ERROR** — a real failure, usually transient (a Smartsheet/Box/network blip);
    daemons retry on their next cycle. Persistent repeats matter, single rows rarely do.
  - **CRITICAL** — the system *paged the operator*: besides this row, a Resend alert
    email and a Sentry event fired. CRITICALs are the "am I on fire" surface.
- **Timestamp** — the occurrence date (a bare date, not a time).
- **Message / Traceback** — the human-readable detail (secrets/PII are redacted
  before the row is written; the raw text stays in the on-Mac log file).
- **Correlation_ID** — a shared ID that **ties this row to the matching Resend alert
  email and Sentry event** for the same incident. If you have an alert email in hand,
  searching ITS_Errors for its correlation ID finds the exact row (and vice versa).
- **Resolved At / Resolved By / Surfaced At / Notes** — operator-triage cells.
  **`Resolved At` is the one that matters mechanically** (next section).

A repeating fault does not email you every cycle — alert dedupe and a capped
re-notify ladder throttle the *pages* — but the rows keep landing, so the sheet is the
honest record of how long a fault has been going on.

## What an "open CRITICAL" is and why it matters

An **open CRITICAL** is a row with `Severity = CRITICAL` and a **blank `Resolved At`**.
Blank means "nobody has dealt with this yet". This is load-bearing, not cosmetic:

- **Watchdog Check B** counts open CRITICALs every morning and WARNs while any exist.
- The **operator dashboard home page** shows them in the open-CRITICALs panel.
- The automatic row-cap rotation (watchdog Check O) and the dashboard clear verb
  **never delete an open CRITICAL** — an unresolved CRITICAL is undeletable by design,
  so a backlog of them can eventually crowd the sheet toward its row cap.
- The log-rotation lane (watchdog Check W) holds part of its work while an open
  CRITICAL is present (incident-safety hold).

So the triage loop is: **fix or escalate the underlying fault first, then mark the
CRITICAL resolved, then (optionally) clear**. Marking resolved without addressing the
cause just blinds the "am I on fire" surface — don't.

## Procedure

### The low-class repair 1 — mark open CRITICALs resolved (dashboard errors/resolve)

Where: dashboard **`/config`** page → **"Error log (Class B · elevated)"** section →
the *mark resolved* form (route `POST /act/errors/resolve`).

1. **A Script and/or Error-code filter is REQUIRED** (both are exact matches against
   the row's `Script` / `Error` cells). An unfiltered mass-resolve is refused by the
   code — you resolve a *known, handled class* (a fixed error code, a retired daemon's
   script), never "everything".
2. **Click *preview* first.** Preview is a dry run: it counts the matching open
   CRITICALs and writes nothing, so you can check the blast radius (a Script filter
   alone sweeps every error code under that daemon) before committing.
3. Commit with the elevated ceremony: re-enter the PIN + type `mark-resolved`.
4. The verb stamps `Resolved At` (today's date) on the matching **open CRITICALs
   only** — WARN/ERROR/INFO rows and already-resolved CRITICALs are never touched, so
   re-running is harmless. Large sets are capped per run (~4,600 rows); run again to
   continue. An audit row (`errors_resolved_marked`) records who did it.

> "Claude, the `<error code>` CRITICALs from `<script>` are handled — walk me through
> previewing and then marking them resolved on the dashboard, and confirm Check B goes
> quiet tomorrow."

### The low-class repair 2 — clear terminal rows (dashboard errors/clear)

Where: same dashboard section → the *clear error log* form (route
`POST /act/errors/clear`), elevated phrase `clear-error-log`.

- Deletes **TERMINAL** rows only: every INFO/WARN/ERROR row, and CRITICALs whose
  `Resolved At` is set. **An open CRITICAL is never touched** — the clear verb reuses
  the same terminality rule as the automatic rotation, so the two can never disagree.
- Optional "older than N days" keeps recent rows; blank means every terminal row.
- **Irreversible** — cleared rows are gone from the forensic log (the clear/rotation
  audit-trail rows themselves are preserved). Clear only what mark-resolved (or time)
  has made terminal and you no longer need; the automatic Check O rotation handles
  routine volume on its own.

### Escalate-to-Seth condition

- Anything in the four FIXED high-capability classes (Op Stds §44) — the **External
  Send Gate, secrets/auth, doctrine, or code changes** — regardless of how well the
  symptom is documented. Reading ITS_Errors is always fine; *fixing* a token, a send
  path, or code never is.
- **A CRITICAL that re-fires after its documented repair** (you re-ran the daemon /
  cleared the flag / fixed the data and the same code came back) — do not loop; do not
  mark it resolved again to silence it.
- **Any error code not covered by a documented symptom** in a runbook (novel = Seth,
  per the both-rule).
- A row whose Message names HMAC, tampering, MALICIOUS, or a security flag — that is
  security territory even when a low-class verb *could* touch the row.

## Owner

`@solutionsmith`. Per-daemon symptom → repair blocks live in that daemon's own §43
runbook (see `docs/runbooks/`); this entry owns the cross-cutting sheet semantics and
the two dashboard verbs.
